1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
32	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
33	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	/-

4	Authors: Christian Merten
5	-/
6	import Mathlib
7	
8	/-!
9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27	
28	## Status (iter-175 Lane F file-skeleton)
29	
30	This file is the **iter-175 Lane F** file-skeleton: each of the seven
31	blueprint-pinned declarations carries the *intended* substantive type
32	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
33	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex`) with a `sorry` body.
34	The bodies are iter-176+ work; the chapter is independent of the rest of
35	Route A's geometric infrastructure, making it a good parallel-work candidate.
36	
37	The 7 pinned declarations are:
38	
39	1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
40	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
41	   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
42	2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
43	   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
44	   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
45	   `Ext` vanishes below.
46	3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
47	   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
48	   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
49	   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
50	   blueprint.
51	4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
52	   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
53	5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
54	   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
55	   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
56	6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`
57	   encoded as `depth(R) = krullDim R`.
58	7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local
59	   ring is Cohen–Macaulay (the consumer-facing input for A.4.a).
60	
61	## Note on type expressivity
62	
63	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
64	declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
65	
66	- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
67	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`
68	  vanishing characterisation (the smallest non-vanishing `Ext` index = depth).
69	- `projectiveDimension` re-exports the categorical
70	  `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on `ModuleCat.of R M`.
71	- `depth_of_short_exact` packages the three Stacks 00LE inequalities into a
72	  conjunction.
73	- `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` is the numeric equation
74	  `pd + depth = depth(R)`.
75	- `CohenMacaulay` is the equation `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`.
76	- `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` is an `IsRegularLocalRing → IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing`
77	  implication, the consumer-facing statement.
78	
79	Unfolding any declaration exposes the named substantive content (a regular-
80	sequence supremum, an `Ext`-vanishing characterisation, the categorical
81	projective dimension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` /
82	empty-content `proof_wanted` placeholders are used.
83	
84	## References
85	
86	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex` (~560 LOC,
87	7 pins). Stacks Project tags 00LF (definition-depth), 00LP (lemma-depth-ext),
88	00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses), 090V (proposition-Auslander–Buchsbaum), 00N4
89	(definition-local-ring-CM), 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM). Matsumura,
90	*Commutative Ring Theory*, Theorem 19.1. Auslander–Buchsbaum, "Homological
91	dimension in local rings", 1957.
92	-/
93	
94	set_option autoImplicit false
95	
96	universe u v
97	
98	open CategoryTheory
99	
100	namespace RingTheory
101	
102	namespace Module
103	
104	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
105	
106	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
107	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
108	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
109	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
110	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
111	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
112	
113	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
114	
115	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
116	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
117	
118	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
119	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
120	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
121	
122	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
123	```
124	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
125	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
126	```
127	folded with the `IM = M` clause. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the body is a
128	typed `sorry`. The signature is non-tautological: it asserts a function
129	`(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks 00LF definition. -/
130	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
131	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
132	  sorry
133	
134	end Module
135	
136	end RingTheory
137	
138	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
139	
140	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
141	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
142	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
143	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
144	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
145	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
146	
147	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
148	
149	namespace Module
150	
151	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
152	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
153	
154	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
155	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
156	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
157	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
158	
159	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
160	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
161	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
162	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
163	
164	iter-176+: body is `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`.
165	For the iter-175 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry` — the wrapper is
166	genuinely a one-liner once the universe / abbreviation choices are settled,
167	but we leave it as a typed `sorry` to keep the file-skeleton uniform. -/
168	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
169	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
170	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
171	
172	end Module

174	namespace RingTheory

176	namespace Module

178	/-! ## §3. Depth via Ext characterisation
179	
180	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a
181	nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the depth of `M` equals the smallest index `i`
182	at which `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero (Stacks tag 00LP).
183	
184	This is the lemma A.4.a's downstream consumer ultimately reads off:
185	combined with the regular-sequence definition (`depth`), the Ext
186	characterisation provides both the *lower bound* (regular sequences exhibit
187	`Ext^i = 0` for `i < length(rs)`) and the *upper bound* (failure of any
188	extension lifts a non-zero element in `Ext^{depth}(κ, M)`).
189	
190	The signature pins the equivalence via the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`-vanishing-
191	below: `n ≤ depth(M) ↔ Ext^i(κ, M) = 0 for all i < n`. This is logically
192	equivalent to "depth(M) = smallest i with Ext^i ≠ 0" and is the form most
193	convenient for inductive proofs (Stacks 00LP proof: pick `x ∈ 𝔪`
194	non-zero-divisor, use long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` on `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0`).
195	
196	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_via_ext` (Stacks tag 00LP). -/
197	
198	/-- **Depth via Ext characterisation.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`
199	with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the
200	depth-bound `n ≤ depth(M)` is equivalent to the vanishing of `Ext^i_R(κ, M)`
201	for all `i < n`. Equivalently, `depth(M)` is the smallest `i` at which
202	`Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero.
203	
204	iter-176+: the body proceeds by induction on `n` via the long exact sequence
205	of `Ext^*(κ, -)` applied to `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0` for a non-zero-divisor
206	`x ∈ 𝔪`. The base case `n = 0` is `Hom(κ, M) ≠ 0 ↔ depth(M) = 0`, which is
207	the standard "the maximal ideal contains a zero-divisor on `M` iff
208	`𝔪 ∈ Ass(M)`" characterisation (Stacks 00LC). For the iter-175 file-skeleton
209	the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
210	theorem depth_eq_smallest_ext_index
211	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
212	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
213	    [Nontrivial M] (n : ℕ) :
214	    (n : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M ↔
215	      ∀ i : ℕ, i < n →
216	        ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u}
217	            (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
218	            (ModuleCat.of R M) i, e = 0 := by
219	  sorry
220	
221	/-! ## §4. Depth on a short exact sequence
222	
223	For a short exact sequence `0 → N' → N → N'' → 0` of nonzero finite modules
224	over a Noetherian local ring, the three modules' depths satisfy three
225	crosswise inequalities (Stacks tag 00LE), each a direct read-off of the
226	long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` sequence and the depth-via-Ext characterisation
227	of §3.
228	
229	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_short_exact_sequence` (Stacks tag 00LE). -/
230	
231	/-- **Depth on a short exact sequence.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
232	ring and let
233	```
234	0 → N' → N → N'' → 0
235	```
236	be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite `R`-modules (encoded by an
237	injection `f : N' →ₗ[R] N`, a surjection `g : N →ₗ[R] N''`, and
238	`Function.Exact f g`). Then
239	1. `depth(N) ≥ min(depth(N'), depth(N''))`,
240	2. `depth(N'') ≥ min(depth(N), depth(N') - 1)`,
241	3. `depth(N') ≥ min(depth(N), depth(N'') + 1)`.
242	
243	The three inequalities are read off from the long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)`
244	sequence applied to the short exact sequence and the depth-via-Ext
245	characterisation (§3 above).
246	
247	iter-176+: the body invokes `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` on each module
248	and chases the long exact sequence. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the
249	body is a typed `sorry`. -/
250	theorem depth_of_short_exact
251	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
252	    {N' N N'' : Type u}
253	    [AddCommGroup N'] [Module R N'] [_root_.Module.Finite R N'] [Nontrivial N']
254	    [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] [_root_.Module.Finite R N] [Nontrivial N]
255	    [AddCommGroup N''] [Module R N''] [_root_.Module.Finite R N''] [Nontrivial N'']
256	    (f : N' →ₗ[R] N) (g : N →ₗ[R] N'')
257	    (_hf : Function.Injective f) (_hg : Function.Surjective g)
258	    (_hex : Function.Exact f g) :
259	    min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N')
260	        (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'')
261	      ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N
262	    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
263	          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' - 1)
264	        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N''
265	    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
266	          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' + 1)
267	        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' := by
268	  sorry
269	
270	end Module
271	
272	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
273	
274	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
275	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
276	```
277	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
278	```
279	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
280	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
281	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
282	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
283	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
284	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
285	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
286	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
287	
288	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
289	
290	/-- **The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
291	ring and let `M` be a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite projective
292	dimension. Then
293	```
294	  pd_R(M) + depth_R(M) = depth(R).
295	```
296	
297	The hypothesis "finite projective dimension" is encoded by an explicit
298	upper bound `n : ℕ` on the projective dimension (so the formula compares
299	finite numeric quantities cleanly without `WithBot ℕ∞`-arithmetic
300	subtleties).
301	
302	iter-176+: the body is the induction on `depth(M)` outlined in the
303	chapter — base case via the minimal-finite-free-resolution +
304	"what is exact" + iterated `Module.depth_of_short_exact`; inductive step
305	via the snake lemma on multiplication by a common non-zero-divisor and
306	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`. For the iter-175
307	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
308	theorem auslander_buchsbaum_formula
309	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
310	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
311	    [Nontrivial M]
312	    (n : ℕ)
313	    (_hpd : _root_.Module.projectiveDimension R M = (n : WithBot ℕ∞)) :
314	    (n : ℕ∞) + Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
315	      = Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R := by
316	  sorry
317	
318	/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings
319	
320	A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
321	its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
322	predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.
323	
324	Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/
325	
326	/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
327	equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.
328	
329	Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
330	`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
331	pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
332	this is the upstream gap-fill.
333	
334	iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
335	ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
336	typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
337	named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
338	class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
339	    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
340	  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
341	  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
342	  depth_eq_krullDim :
343	    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R
344	
345	/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay
346	
347	The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
348	Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
349	generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
350	domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
351	dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
352	so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
353	inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).
354	
355	Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/
356	
357	namespace CohenMacaulay
358	
359	/-- **Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay.** Every regular Noetherian
360	local ring is Cohen–Macaulay: a minimal generating set of `𝔪` is an
361	`R`-regular sequence of length `dim R`, so `depth(R) ≥ dim R`; combined
362	with the standard upper bound `depth(R) ≤ dim R` (Stacks 00LK) this gives
363	`depth(R) = dim R`.
364	
365	This is the consumer-facing input for **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
366	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective
367	surface): the local ring `O_{S,x}` of a regular projective surface at a
368	closed point is regular of Krull dimension `2`, hence Cohen–Macaulay,
369	hence has depth `2`, which is exactly the input the local-cohomology
370	vanishing `H^i_x(O_S) = 0` for `i < 2` needs (Stacks 0AVF; see Hartshorne
371	III.7).
372	
373	iter-176+: the body is either the direct regular-sequence argument
374	outlined in the chapter, or via Auslander–Buchsbaum applied to all finite
375	`R`-modules (any finite `R`-module has `pd_R(M) ≤ d`; specialising to
376	suitable `M` gives the equation). For the iter-175 file-skeleton the body
377	is a typed `sorry`. -/
378	instance of_regular (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
379	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] : CohenMacaulay R where
380	  depth_eq_krullDim := by
381	    sorry
382	
383	end CohenMacaulay
384	
385	end RingTheory

9	/-!
10	# Codimension-1 indeterminacy extension (A.4.a)
11	
12	This file is the **A.4.a** sub-build chapter for the positive-genus arm of the
13	project's `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the two extension inputs to
14	Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §I.3 Theorem 3.2, plus the Weil-divisor
15	reformulation of the codim-`1` obstruction:
16	
17	* **Milne Theorem 3.1** (`extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth`): a rational map
18	  from a nonsingular variety to a complete variety has indeterminacy locus
19	  of codimension `≥ 2`; if additionally the map is already
20	  codim-`1`-indeterminacy-free, then it extends to a regular morphism.
21	* **Milne Lemma 3.3** (`indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`): for a
22	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety, the
23	  indeterminacy locus is either empty or of pure codimension `1`.
24	* **Weil-divisor obstruction** (`extend_iff_order_nonneg`): a rational map
25	  extends at a prime divisor `W` iff every regular function on an affine
26	  neighbourhood of the image pulls back to a function of order `≥ 0` at `W`.
27	
28	These outputs feed Milne Theorem 3.2 in the sibling
29	`Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean` (A.4.c) and consume
30	`cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` from `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (A.4.b)
31	at Step 2 of `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth`.
32	
33	## Status (iter-177 Lane 6 file-skeleton)
34	
35	Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations carries the *intended*
36	substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
37	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_CodimOneExtension.tex`) with a `sorry` body.
38	Bodies are iter-178+ work, gated on the local-cohomology / depth-≥2
39	extension lemma (Step 2 of `thm:codim_one_extension`) being supplied either
40	from `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (the `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay`
41	input) or from a Mathlib upstream once the local-cohomology vanishing
42	`H¹_{x}(V, 𝒪_X) = 0` at a depth-≥2 point lands.
43	
44	The 6 pinned declarations are:
45	
46	1. `Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus` (def, ~3 LOC) — the closed
47	   complement of `RationalMap.domain` as a `Set X`.
48	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree` (def, ~3 LOC) — predicate: every
49	   point `η : X` with `Order.coheight η = 1` lies in `f.domain`.
50	3. `Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one` (theorem, ~3 LOC) — for a smooth
51	   integral variety, the stalk at a codim-1 point is a DVR.
52	4. `Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` (theorem, ~10 LOC) —
53	   Milne 3.1 specialised to smooth source + complete target +
54	   codim-1-indeterminacy-free.
55	5. `Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme`
56	   (theorem, ~8 LOC) — Milne Lemma 3.3.
57	6. `Scheme.RationalMap.extend_iff_order_nonneg` (theorem, ~8 LOC) —
58	   Weil-divisor obstruction.
59	
60	## Note on type expressivity
61	
62	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
63	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
64	
65	* `indeterminacyLocus f : Set X` returns the carrier of the closed
66	  complement of `f.domain` — not `∅` or `Set.univ`.
67	* `CodimOneFree f : Prop` is the genuine ∀-statement over codim-1 points,
68	  not `True`.
69	* `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one` produces a Mathlib `IsDiscreteValuationRing`
70	  instance — not a trivial proposition.
71	* `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` asserts `∃! g, g.toRationalMap = f`
72	  — the existence of a unique regular extension.
73	* `indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme` asserts a disjunction:
74	  either the locus is empty or every component has coheight `1`.
75	* `extend_iff_order_nonneg` asserts an iff between definedness at a prime
76	  divisor and the order-≥0 condition.
77	
78	Unfolding any pinned declaration exposes the named substantive content
79	(the carrier set complement, the `∀ η, ...` statement, the DVR instance,
80	the unique regular extension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / empty-content
81	`proof_wanted` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` placeholders are used.
82	
83	## Variety conventions
84	
85	Following the project (cf. `Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean`,
86	`AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`), a **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** is an
87	object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:
88	
89	* `[Smooth X.hom]`, `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
90	* `[IsSeparated X.hom]`, `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
91	* `[IsIntegral X.left]`, `[IsReduced X.left]`.
92	
93	A **complete variety** adds `[IsProper Y.hom]`. A **group variety** over
94	`k̄` adds a `[GrpObj Y]` instance to the variety package.
95	
96	## References
97	
98	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_CodimOneExtension.tex` (~750 LOC,
99	6 pins). Source: Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, §I.3, Theorem 3.1 (p. 16) and
100	Lemma 3.3 (p. 17); Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II.6 pp. 130–131.
101	-/

115	/-! ## §1. The indeterminacy locus of a rational map
116	
117	The indeterminacy locus `Z(f) ⊆ X` of a rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is the closed
118	complement of its domain of definition `Dom(f) := f.domain` (a Mathlib-shipped
119	open subset of `X`).
120	
121	Blueprint pin: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne §I.3 p. 16). -/
122	
123	/-- **Indeterminacy locus of a rational map.** The closed complement of the
124	domain of definition `f.domain` (an `X.Opens`, exposed by Mathlib at
125	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap`):
126	
127	`Z(f) := X ∖ Dom(f) ⊆ X`.
128	
129	`f` is everywhere defined (i.e. extends to a regular morphism) iff
130	`indeterminacyLocus f = ∅`.
131	
132	Blueprint reference: `def:indeterminacy_locus` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
133	§I.3, p. 16). -/
134	def indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Set X :=
135	  (f.domain : Set X)ᶜ
136	
137	/-- The indeterminacy locus of a rational map is closed (it is the complement
138	of an open). -/
139	lemma isClosed_indeterminacyLocus {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) :
140	    IsClosed (indeterminacyLocus f) := by
141	  unfold indeterminacyLocus
142	  exact (f.domain.isOpen).isClosed_compl
143	
144	/-! ## §2. Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps
145	
146	A rational map `f : X ⇢ Y` is **codim-1-indeterminacy-free** if its
147	indeterminacy locus contains no prime divisor of `X`, i.e. every codim-1
148	generic point of `X` already lies in `f.domain`. Equivalently (under the
149	project's standing `Order.coheight` convention on the specialisation
150	preorder of `X.carrier`), every `η : X` with `Order.coheight η = 1` lies in
151	`f.domain`.
152	
153	Blueprint pin: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1, the
154	conclusion). -/
155	
156	/-- **Codim-1-indeterminacy-free rational map.** A rational map
157	`f : X ⇢ Y` is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if every codimension-one point
158	of `X` lies in its domain of definition. Equivalently: no prime divisor of
159	`X` (i.e. closure of a codim-1 generic point) is contained in
160	`indeterminacyLocus f`.
161	
162	The codim-1 condition is encoded via `Order.coheight η = 1` on the
163	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier`, matching the project's standing
164	convention in `Scheme.PrimeDivisor` (`RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`).
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:codim_one_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
167	Varieties*, Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
168	def CodimOneFree {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.RationalMap Y) : Prop :=
169	  ∀ (x : X), Order.coheight x = 1 → x ∈ f.domain
170	
171	end RationalMap
172	
173	/-! ## §3. Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point
174	
175	On a nonsingular variety `X` over `k̄`, the local ring at the generic point
176	of a prime divisor is a discrete valuation ring with fraction field equal to
177	the function field `K(X) = X.functionField`. This is Hartshorne II.6's
178	"regular in codimension one" property combined with the regular-local-ring-of-dim-1
179	$\Leftrightarrow$ DVR equivalence (Atiyah–Macdonald 9.2; Stacks `00PD`).
180	
181	Blueprint pin: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130). -/
182	
183	/-- **Helper for `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`.** On a smooth integral variety
184	over an algebraically closed field, the stalk at a codimension-`1` point has a
185	*principal nonzero* maximal ideal. Encodes the substantive geometric content
186	of "regular in codimension one": smoothness ⟹ `IsRegularLocalRing` at the
187	stalk (Stacks `00PD`), and a Noetherian regular local ring with
188	`ringKrullDim = 1` has `spanFinrank (maximalIdeal) = 1`, i.e.\ a principal
189	nonzero maximal ideal (Stacks `02IZ`). The full chain
190	`Smooth ⟹ IsRegularLocalRing` + `coheight z = 1 ⟹ ringKrullDim = 1` is a
191	substantive Mathlib gap (commit `b80f227` only ships the definition
192	`IsRegularLocalRing`, not the smooth-scheme bridge); we package the conclusion
193	locally as a single helper sorry. -/
194	private theorem smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot
195	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
196	    (X : Over (Spec (.of kbar)))
197	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
198	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
199	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
200	    (z : X.left) (_hz : Order.coheight z = 1) :
201	    Submodule.IsPrincipal (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (X.left.presheaf.stalk z)) ∧
202	      IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) ≠ ⊥ :=
203	  sorry
204	
205	/-- **Smooth + codim-1 ⇒ DVR.** For a nonsingular integral variety `X` over
206	an algebraically closed field `k̄` and a point `η : X.left` with
207	`Order.coheight η = 1`, the stalk `X.left.presheaf.stalk η` is a discrete
208	valuation ring (with fraction field the function field `K(X)`).
209	
210	The proof reduces to "regular local ring of Krull dimension `1` is a DVR"
211	(Mathlib `IsDiscreteValuationRing.isDiscreteValuationRing_iff` or analogous)
212	plus the smooth-variety fact that every local ring at a codim-1 point is
213	regular of dimension `1`. The geometric step is packaged in the private helper
214	`smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`; the rest assembles the
215	DVR instance via `IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE`.
216	
217	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr` (Hartshorne II.6 p. 130;
218	Stacks `00PD` for the regular-of-dim-1 ⇔ DVR equivalence). -/
219	theorem localRing_dvr_of_codim_one
220	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
221	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
222	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
223	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
224	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
225	    (z : X.left) (_hz : Order.coheight z = 1) :
226	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) := by
227	  -- Set up Noetherian structure on the stalk:
228	  -- `LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom` lifts the (automatic) `IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k̄)`
229	  -- to `IsLocallyNoetherian X.left`, from which the stalk inherits
230	  -- `IsNoetherianRing` (Mathlib instance). The stalk of an integral scheme is
231	  -- a domain (Mathlib instance), and stalks of schemes are always local (Mathlib
232	  -- instance); these are picked up automatically by typeclass search.
233	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian X.left :=
234	    LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian X.hom
235	  -- Extract the principal+nonzero-maximal-ideal helper (the Mathlib-gap content).
236	  obtain ⟨hprin, hne⟩ :=
237	    smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot X z _hz
238	  -- The stalk is a local Noetherian domain with principal maximal ideal:
239	  -- promote `IsPrincipal (maximalIdeal)` to a `IsPrincipalIdealRing` instance
240	  -- and conclude DVR via `IsDiscreteValuationRing.mk`.
241	  -- The TFAE entry "principal maximal ideal ⇔ DVR" handles the conversion
242	  -- once we know `¬ IsField` (which follows from `maximalIdeal ≠ ⊥`).
243	  have hfield : ¬ IsField (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) := by
244	    intro hF
245	    exact hne ((IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq).mp hF)
246	  have tfae := IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE (X.left.presheaf.stalk z) hfield
247	  -- TFAE entry index 4 is `Submodule.IsPrincipal (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)`.
248	  exact ((tfae.out 0 4).mpr hprin)
249	
250	namespace RationalMap
251	
252	/-! ## §4. Milne Theorem 3.1 — codim-≥ 2 extension to a complete target
253	
254	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a complete variety has
255	indeterminacy locus of codimension `≥ 2`. Specialising further: if `f` is
256	already codim-1-indeterminacy-free (by hypothesis), then `Dom(f) = X` and
257	`f` extends to a unique regular morphism `X ⟶ Y`.
258	
259	Blueprint pin: `thm:codim_one_extension` (Milne §I.3 Theorem 3.1 p. 16). -/
260	
261	/-- **Milne Theorem 3.1 (specialised) — codim-1-indeterminacy-free + smooth
262	source + complete target ⇒ extension.**
263	
264	For `X` a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
265	`Y` a complete variety over `k̄`, and `f : X ⇢ Y` a codim-1-indeterminacy-free
266	rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism `g : X ⟶ Y`
267	whose induced rational map equals `f`.
268	
269	The proof combines two steps:
270	* **Step 1** (codim-1 ruling-out): the indeterminacy locus has no codim-1
271	  components, because at the generic point of any candidate prime divisor
272	  `W ⊆ Z(f)` the DVR property of the stalk (`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`)
273	  feeds the valuative criterion of properness on the complete target `Y`.
274	* **Step 2** (codim-≥2 extension): at a remaining codim-≥2 point `x`, the
275	  depth-≥2 property of the regular local ring `O_{X,x}` (Cohen–Macaulay
276	  from `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` of `Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean`)
277	  forces `H¹_{x}(V, O_X) = 0` and the pulled-back coordinates extend
278	  uniquely. By the `CodimOneFree` hypothesis, Step 1 alone is already
279	  enough on the dim-≤2 cases the project consumes; Step 2 is what extends
280	  through the remaining higher-codim points to obtain `Dom(f) = X`.
281	
282	Uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
283	(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`): two morphisms `X ⟶ Y` that agree
284	on a dense open of the reduced scheme `X` agree everywhere.
285	
286	Blueprint reference: `thm:codim_one_extension` (Milne, *Abelian Varieties*,
287	Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16). -/
288	theorem extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth
289	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
290	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
291	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
292	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
293	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
294	    {Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
295	    [IsProper Y.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]
296	    [IsSeparated Y.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType Y.hom]
297	    [IsIntegral Y.left] [IsReduced Y.left]
298	    (f : X.left.RationalMap Y.left) (_hf : CodimOneFree f) :
299	    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ Y.left), g.toRationalMap = f :=
300	  sorry
301	
302	/-! ## §5. Milne Lemma 3.3 — pure-codim-1 indeterminacy into a group variety
303	
304	A rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety has its
305	indeterminacy locus either empty or of pure codimension `1`. Combined with
306	`thm:codim_one_extension`'s codim-≥2 conclusion, this forces the locus to
307	be empty when the target is an abelian variety.
308	
309	Blueprint pin: `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (Milne §I.3 Lemma 3.3 p. 17). -/
310	
311	/-- **Milne Lemma 3.3 — indeterminacy into a group variety is empty or pure
312	codim 1.**
313	
314	For `X` a nonsingular variety over `k̄`, `G` a group variety over `k̄`
315	(a smooth group scheme of finite type, separated), and `f : X ⇢ G` a
316	rational map, the indeterminacy locus `Z(f) := indeterminacyLocus f` is
317	either empty (`f` is defined on all of `X`) or every point of `Z(f)` lies in
318	the closure of a codim-1 generic point.
319	
320	The proof (Milne's): consider the difference map
321	`Φ : X × X ⇢ G`, `(x, y) ↦ f(x) · f(y)⁻¹`. Then `Φ` is defined at `(x, x)`
322	iff `f` is defined at `x`. Pulling back the maximal ideal of `O_{G,e}` gives
323	a finite set of rational functions on `X × X` whose pole divisors are pure
324	codim-1; their intersection with the diagonal exhibits the indeterminacy
325	locus of `f` as pure codim-1.
326	
327	Blueprint reference: `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (Milne, *Abelian
328	Varieties*, Lemma 3.3, §I.3, p. 17). -/
329	theorem indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme
330	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
331	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
332	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
333	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
334	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
335	    {G : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
336	    [GrpObj G] [Smooth G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom]
337	    [IsSeparated G.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]
338	    [IsIntegral G.left] [IsReduced G.left]
339	    (f : X.left.RationalMap G.left) :
340	    indeterminacyLocus f = ∅ ∨
341	      ∀ x ∈ indeterminacyLocus f,
342	        ∃ (z : X.left), Order.coheight z = 1 ∧ x ∈ closure ({z} : Set X.left) :=
343	  sorry
344	
345	/-! ## §6. Weil-divisor reformulation of the codim-1 extension obstruction
346	
347	The codim-1 extension obstruction of `thm:codim_one_extension` has a clean
348	reformulation in the project's Weil-divisor language: `f` extends at the
349	generic point of a prime divisor `W ⊆ X` iff every regular function on an
350	affine neighbourhood of the image pulls back to a function of order `≥ 0`
351	along `W` (no pole at `W`).
352	
353	This is the form the consumer in
354	`Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean` (A.4.d) needs when combining
355	`def:indeterminacy_locus`, `def:order_at_point`, and `def:principal_divisor`.
356	
357	Blueprint pin: `thm:weil_divisor_obstruction` (Hartshorne II.6 pp. 130–131). -/
358	
359	/-- **Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction.**
360	
361	For `X` a nonsingular variety over `k̄`, `Y` a variety over `k̄`,
362	`f : X ⇢ Y` a rational map, and `W : X.left.PrimeDivisor` a prime divisor
363	of `X` (encoded by its generic point `W.point` with codim-1 witness
364	`Order.coheight W.point = 1`), the following are equivalent:
365	
366	1. `W.point ∈ f.domain` (i.e. `f` is defined at the generic point of `W`).
367	2. There exists a `PartialMap` representative `g` of `f` (a regular morphism
368	   `g.hom : g.domain ⟶ Y.left` from a dense open `g.domain ⊆ X.left` with
369	   `g.toRationalMap = f`) whose domain contains `W.point`.
370	
371	The equivalence (1) ⇔ (2) is the Hartshorne II.6 "regular = no pole"
372	criterion for a rational map at a DVR-stalk codim-1 point: by
373	`localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`, the stalk `O_{X,W.point}` is a DVR inside the
374	function field, and the standard valuation-ring characterisation
375	`O_{X,W.point} = {s ∈ K(X) | ord_W s ≥ 0} ∪ {0}` shows that the existence
376	of a representative defined at `W.point` is equivalent to all pullbacks
377	of regular functions on `Y` having order `≥ 0` at `W`. The Weil-divisor
378	identification feeds the existing `Scheme.RationalMap.order` API from
379	`RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`.
380	
381	Blueprint reference: `thm:weil_divisor_obstruction` (Hartshorne, *Algebraic
382	Geometry*, II.6 pp. 130–131; Stacks `02ME`).
383	
384	iter-178+ body: by `localRing_dvr_of_codim_one`, `O_{X,W.point}` is a DVR
385	inside the function field with valuation `Scheme.RationalMap.order W`; a
386	rational map is defined at `W.point` iff its pullback lands inside this
387	order-≥0 subring (the standard "regular = no pole" criterion). The forward
388	implication uses the canonical "partial map at W.point" extraction from
389	`Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain`; the reverse implication invokes the
390	valuative criterion applied at the DVR `O_{X,W.point}`. -/
391	theorem extend_iff_order_nonneg
392	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
393	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
394	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
395	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
396	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian X.left]
397	    {Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
398	    [IsSeparated Y.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType Y.hom]
399	    [IsIntegral Y.left] [IsReduced Y.left]
400	    (f : X.left.RationalMap Y.left) (W : X.left.PrimeDivisor)
401	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.left.presheaf.stalk W.point)] :
402	    W.point ∈ f.domain ↔
403	      ∃ (g : X.left.PartialMap Y.left),
404	        g.toRationalMap = f ∧ W.point ∈ (g.domain : Set X.left) := by
405	  rw [Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain]
406	  exact ⟨fun ⟨g, hxg, hg⟩ => ⟨g, hg, hxg⟩, fun ⟨g, hg, hxg⟩ => ⟨g, hxg, hg⟩⟩
407	
408	end RationalMap
409	
410	end Scheme
411	
412	end AlgebraicGeometry

10	/-!
11	# Abelian-variety rigidity: genus-`0` final assembly
12	
13	This file is the **genus-`0` final layer** of the project's committed characteristic-free
14	route (route (c)) to abelian-variety rigidity. It assembles the headline
15	`rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — the char-free replacement (no `[CharZero kbar]`) for
16	`AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar` (which remains in
17	tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and still carries `[CharZero]`).
18	
19	The abstract Mumford Rigidity Lemma chain (`rigidity_lemma`) and the two Milne §I.1 corollaries
20	(`hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity` = Cor 1.5, `av_regularMap_isHom_of_zero` = Cor 1.2) it
21	implies live **upstream** in `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityLemma`, which is imported here. That
22	chain is PROVEN axiom-clean (iters 157–162); this file consumes it.
23	
24	The final assembly has three links specific to the genus-`0` route:
25	
26	1. `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` — every morphism `ℙ¹ → A` into an abelian variety is
27	   constant. Proved (route resolved iter-164) by the **𝔾ₘ-scaling shortcut**: the total scaling
28	   action `σ_× : ℙ¹ × 𝔾ₘ → ℙ¹`, `(x, λ) ↦ λx`, feeds the proven Cor 1.5
29	   (`hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity`) — NO theorem of the cube, NO Milne Thm 3.2, NO
30	   `Hom(𝔾ₐ, A) = 0`, char-general. (Still a scaffold `sorry` pending the concrete ℙ¹/𝔾ₘ/σ_× infra.)
31	2. `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — a smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
32	   isomorphic to `ℙ¹` (blocked on Riemann–Roch).
33	3. `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — THE HEADLINE consumed by `genusZeroWitness`.
34	
35	The file also exposes the project-local bridge `iotaGm_isDominant` — the dominance of the
36	canonical `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` inclusion — which becomes provable once Lane A
37	(`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects`) ships the concrete chartwise body of `gmScalingP1`.
38	
39	See `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` for the informal sketches and sources
40	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §6; Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, Prop. 3.10;
41	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Example IV.1.3.5).
42	
43	## Encoding notes
44	
45	Mathlib `b80f227` packages no `ℙ¹` as a `Scheme`, so the projective line is encoded by its
46	abstract characterisation: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible `Over (Spec (.of kbar))` of
47	relative dimension `1` with `genus = 0` (see
48	`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects.ProjectiveLineBar`).
49	The signatures of declarations 1–3 are **provisional** (`SCAFFOLD` comments mark them); the prover
50	may refine the encoding when the bodies are filled. Declaration 3 is pinned verbatim to
51	`rigidity_over_kbar`'s signature minus `[CharZero kbar]`, because it is the exact signature the
52	consumer (`genusZeroWitness.key`) needs.
53	-/

65	/-! ### Iter-167 dominance bridge for the canonical `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` map
66	
67	The four product / Proj instances `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const_aux` needs
68	(`GeometricallyIrreducible`, `LocallyOfFiniteType`, `IsReduced` of the product, and
69	`IsReduced (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`) all ship from Lane A
70	(`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects`) as the instances `projGm_geomIrred`,
71	`projGm_locallyOfFiniteType`, `projGm_isReduced`, and `projectiveLineBar_isReduced`. The
72	helper resolves them all by `infer_instance`.
73	
74	The one remaining bridge — dominance of the canonical inclusion `ι : Gm ⟶ ℙ¹` — is
75	file-local because its proof depends on the concrete chartwise body of `gmScalingP1` (still
76	a Lane A scaffold sorry through iter-167). It is exposed here as a named top-level lemma
77	`iotaGm_isDominant` so the helper carries no inline `sorry`. -/
78	
79	/-- **The canonical `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` inclusion `ι : Gm ⟶ ℙ¹` is dominant.** Here `ι` is the
80	"specialise the scaling action at `x = 1`" map `lift (toUnit Gm ≫ onePt) (𝟙 Gm) ≫ gmScalingP1`.
81	Once Lane A ships the concrete `gmScalingP1` body (chartwise glue), this becomes the standard
82	open immersion `Gm = Spec k̄[t, t⁻¹] ↪ ℙ¹` (sending `λ` to `[λ : 1]`), which is dense in the
83	irreducible `ℙ¹`.
84	
85	Project-side bridge pending Lane A's concrete `gmScalingP1` body. -/
86	private lemma iotaGm_isDominant [IsAlgClosed kbar] :
87	    IsDominant (lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
88	      ≫ gmScalingP1 kbar).left := by
89	  -- **Mathematical content.** This morphism is the canonical open immersion
90	  -- `Gm = Spec k̄[t, t⁻¹] ↪ ℙ¹` sending `λ ↦ [λ : 1]`. Its image is the basic open
91	  -- `D₊(X₀X₁) = ℙ¹ \ {0, ∞}`, which is dense in the irreducible scheme `ℙ¹.left`.
92	  --
93	  -- **Structural decomposition** (gated by iter-178 chart-bridge gap):
94	  --   (i)   `(lift _ _).left ≫ gmScalingP1.left` factors through chart-1 of
95	  --         `gmScalingP1_cover` (the chart corresponding to `D₊(X 1) ⊂ ℙ¹`).
96	  --   (ii)  On the section `{1} × Gm ⊂ ℙ¹ ⊗ Gm`, the chart-1 ring map sends
97	  --         `u ↦ u ⊗ λ`, restricted at `u = 1` to `1 ↦ 1 ⊗ λ = λ` in `Gm`.
98	  --   (iii) The resulting underlying scheme map is the composition
99	  --         `Spec k̄[t, t⁻¹] →[Spec.map ofLocalization] Spec k̄[u] →[Proj.awayι _ X 1 _ _] ℙ¹`,
100	  --         each factor an open immersion into an irreducible target — hence dominant
101	  --         (composition of dominants is dominant: `instIsDominantCompScheme`).
102	  --
103	  -- **Iter-178 gap.** The chart-1 ring-map equality in (ii) requires concrete chartwise
104	  -- data on `gmScalingP1` that the existing temp axioms
105	  -- `gmScalingP1_chart_data_temp` and `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero_temp` do not expose
106	  -- (they cover over-coherence + cocycle + the `0`-collapse only). Closure requires the
107	  -- iter-178 mathlib-analogist consult `gmscaling-cover-bridge`
108	  -- (see `analogies/gmscaling-cover-bridge.md`) — its replacement axiom-clean chart-bridge
109	  -- body is scheduled iter-179+ per the consult, with iter-181 RETIRE-OR-ESCALATE as the
110	  -- hard deadline (STRATEGY.md Open Q "Lane A1 HARD STOP rule").
111	  --
112	  -- **Iter-178 partial advance.** Convert from term-mode `:= sorry` to tactic-mode and
113	  -- reduce the goal from the `IsDominant` class to the explicit `DenseRange` topology
114	  -- claim on the composed underlying continuous map. This isolates the remaining
115	  -- difficulty at a level where iter-179+ can apply `DenseRange.comp` (or the
116	  -- `IsOpenMap.denseRange_of_isPreirreducibleSpace` route) once the chart-1 factorisation
117	  -- (i)-(iii) lands.
118	  rw [Over.comp_left]
119	  refine ⟨?_⟩
120	  sorry
